properties.description: The landmark 'Attention Is All You Need' paper replaced recurrent networks entirely with self-attention, enabling full parallelization of sequence processing for the first time. This single architectural leap proved so universally applicable that it now underpins virtually every major language, vision, and multimodal model built since—making it the most influential paper in modern AI history.

GPT-1 established the pre-train-then-fine-tune paradigm: a 117M parameter transformer pre-trained on BookCorpus with next-token prediction, then fine-tuned on a specific task, outperformed architectures designed specifically for those tasks. This simple recipe—unsupervised pre-training captures language structure, supervised fine-tuning applies it—became the dominant approach for the next six years.

BERT upended the pre-training playbook by training bidirectionally—predicting randomly masked tokens using context from both left and right simultaneously. This masked language modeling objective, combined with next-sentence prediction, allowed BERT to set state-of-the-art on 11 NLP benchmarks at once, and launched the era of fine-tuning large pre-trained models for virtually every downstream NLP task.

Scaling GPT-1's recipe to 1.5B parameters and 40GB of curated web text, GPT-2 produced coherent multi-paragraph text so convincingly that OpenAI delayed its full release citing misuse concerns. It demonstrated for the first time that language models could perform rudimentary zero-shot task transfer—summarizing, translating, and answering questions without any task-specific training—foreshadowing the few-shot capabilities that defined GPT-3.

XLNet from CMU and Google Brain addressed BERT's core limitation: because BERT masks tokens during training, it never learns dependencies between masked positions. XLNet solved this with permutation language modeling—training to predict tokens in random order, conditioning each prediction on all other positions. At 340M parameters it set state-of-the-art across 20 NLP tasks, outperforming BERT on every one.

NVIDIA's Megatron-LM provided the first practical blueprint for training models too large to fit on a single GPU, using tensor parallelism to split individual matrix operations across multiple GPUs rather than splitting layers. By enabling training of 8.3B parameter models in 2019, it laid the distributed training foundation that made GPT-3-scale (175B) achievable one year later.

Sparse Transformers made attention on long sequences tractable by restricting each token to attend to only a local window plus strided distant tokens, reducing complexity from O(n²) to O(n√n). Applied to images, audio, and text, it enabled coherent generation of sequences up to 12,288 tokens long and introduced the factored sparse attention patterns that dozens of subsequent efficient transformer designs built upon.

FreeLB (Free Large-Batch adversarial training) applied adversarial perturbations to word embeddings during fine-tuning, improving the robustness and generalization of pre-trained language models. By accumulating gradients over multiple adversarial steps within a single backward pass—making the process computationally 'free'—it improved BERT and RoBERTa performance on GLUE benchmarks without any inference-time overhead.

T5 made a radical but elegant proposal: reframe every NLP task—translation, summarization, classification, Q&A—as a text-to-text problem with a single unified encoder-decoder model. Trained on a curated 750GB 'Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus' (C4), it enabled clean ablation studies of pre-training objectives, and its 11B-parameter variant set records across the board while establishing the text-to-text format as a versatile training paradigm.

ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) solved the central memory bottleneck of large-scale training: by partitioning optimizer states, gradients, and model parameters across data-parallel workers and eliminating all redundancy, it enabled training of models with trillions of parameters on existing hardware. ZeRO became the backbone of Microsoft's DeepSpeed library and powers virtually every frontier training run today.

Kaplan et al. derived clean empirical power laws governing how language model loss scales with parameters, dataset size, and compute budget. Their key finding—that compute should be split roughly equally between model size and data—guided model development for two years, until Chinchilla later revised the optimal ratio toward more data. These scaling laws gave labs a principled roadmap for allocating training budgets without guesswork.

Vision Transformer (ViT) proved that transformers with no convolutional components whatsoever could achieve state-of-the-art image classification when trained on sufficient data—simply patching images into 16×16 sequences and processing them as tokens. While it required more data than CNNs to generalize, ViT launched a complete architectural revolution in computer vision, ultimately replacing convolutional networks in most frontier vision and multimodal systems.

GPT-3's 175B parameters placed it in an entirely different category: with no gradient updates whatsoever, it could perform arithmetic, write code, translate languages, and answer trivia questions simply by reading a few examples in its prompt. This 'few-shot learning' capability made it the first model that non-ML practitioners could use productively, triggered an industry-wide scaling race, and directly seeded the ChatGPT product line.

DALL-E adapted GPT-3's architecture to image generation by treating both text tokens and image tokens as a flat sequence of discrete codes, training a 12B parameter autoregressive transformer to predict image tokens conditioned on text. Trained on 250M text-image pairs from the web, it demonstrated that generative language modeling—with no architectural changes—could produce coherent, imaginative images from arbitrary textual descriptions.

Switch Transformers proved that trillion-parameter models could be trained affordably by replacing every dense feed-forward layer with a sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) layer routing each token to just one expert. A 1.6 trillion parameter Switch Transformer trained on the same compute as a 10B dense model—demonstrating that parameter count and compute need not scale together, and laying the architectural foundation for Mixtral, DeepSeek-V2, and Llama 4.

Fine-tuned from GPT-3 on 54 million public GitHub repositories, Codex was the first genuinely useful AI programming assistant and powered GitHub Copilot. It demonstrated that a model trained on code develops a grasp of logical structure that extends to novel programs—translating natural language specifications into working Python, JavaScript, and a dozen other languages, making AI-assisted programming a mainstream developer tool.

This Stanford report coined the term 'foundation model' and provided the first systematic analysis of what it means to pre-train a massive general model then adapt it downstream. More than a technical paper, it was a sociological intervention—establishing a shared vocabulary, cataloging emergent capabilities and risks of homogenization, and framing the research agenda the field has followed since. It also raised early alarms about concentration, misuse, and alignment that proved prescient.

FLAN (Fine-tuned LAnguage Net) showed that instruction tuning—training a 137B LaMDA model on 62 NLP tasks reformulated as natural language instructions—dramatically improved zero-shot performance on held-out tasks. The key insight: the way models are trained to respond to prompts matters as much as their raw scale. This finding launched the instruction-tuning paradigm that every aligned LLM since has adopted.

T0 extended the FLAN insight to the open-source world, fine-tuning T5 on hundreds of tasks with crowdsourced human-written prompts from the P3 benchmark collection. A key finding: T0 outperformed GPT-3 on several zero-shot benchmarks despite being roughly 16x smaller, demonstrating that instruction diversity could substitute for raw parameter count—an early proof that open models could match proprietary ones with the right training recipe.

GLaM (Generalist Language Model) applied mixture-of-experts at Google scale, training a 1.2 trillion parameter model with 64 experts per layer—activating only 2 per token. Crucially, GLaM matched GPT-3 on most tasks while consuming just one-third the energy to train and requiring far less compute per inference token, making the practical case that MoE architectures could deliver frontier performance at a fraction of the running cost.

WebGPT gave GPT-3 a browser: the model could issue search queries, follow links, and quote retrieved text verbatim before answering a question. Trained with human feedback to prefer factual, well-cited answers, it outperformed the base model on TruthfulQA—an early demonstration that grounding language models in retrieval was a practical path to improved factuality, foreshadowing modern RAG pipelines and tool-using agents.

RETRO (Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer) achieved GPT-3-level language modeling perplexity with only a 7B parameter model by augmenting each forward pass with retrieval over a 2-trillion-token database. Rather than storing all knowledge in weights, RETRO offloaded it to a searchable corpus retrieved at inference time—the architectural ancestor of today's Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems used in nearly every production LLM deployment.

DeepMind's Gopher was a 280B parameter model evaluated exhaustively across 152 diverse tasks—one of the most systematic benchmark evaluations at the time. While it excelled at knowledge-intensive tasks like reading comprehension and fact-checking, it struggled unexpectedly at mathematical and logical reasoning, providing crucial early evidence that scale alone does not uniformly improve all capabilities and that different training objectives are needed for different reasoning types.

Google's Conformer combined convolution (for capturing local acoustic patterns) with self-attention (for long-range context) in a purpose-built speech architecture. By applying multi-head attention and depthwise convolution in a sequential 'sandwich' pattern within each encoder block, Conformer achieved significant word error rate reductions over pure-attention and pure-convolution models and remains the dominant backbone in production speech recognition systems worldwide.

Chain-of-thought prompting showed that simply asking a model to reason step by step before answering—or including worked examples of reasoning in the prompt—dramatically improved performance on multi-step tasks like math word problems and commonsense reasoning. This deceptively simple technique unlocked reasoning capabilities already latent in large models, set off a wave of reasoning research, and became the direct predecessor of o1-style test-time compute scaling.

LaMDA (Language Model for Dialogue Applications) was Google's first model built specifically for open-ended conversation, fine-tuned with human raters on sensibleness, specificity, and safety. It became notorious when a Google engineer publicly claimed it was sentient—a claim that sparked global debate about AI consciousness. LaMDA later became the foundation for Google's Bard chatbot at its rushed February 2023 launch.

Minerva showed that fine-tuning PaLM on a high-quality dataset of mathematical content—LaTeX-formatted arXiv papers plus high-school math textbooks—could dramatically improve quantitative reasoning without architectural changes. It solved 50% of MATH competition problems and 78% of STEM undergraduate problems, demonstrating that domain-targeted training data matters as much as model scale for technical tasks.

The joint Microsoft-NVIDIA collaboration produced Megatron-Turing NLG, a 530B parameter dense model that set zero-shot generalization records in early 2022. It demonstrated that combining NVIDIA's Megatron tensor parallelism with Microsoft's DeepSpeed pipeline parallelism could reliably train models at scales previously considered impractical, advancing the distributed training toolkit that the entire field now relies on.

BEST-RQ (BERT-based Speech pre-Training with Random-projection Quantizer) pre-trains a speech encoder using masked prediction: audio frames are quantized into discrete targets using a random, unlearned projection matrix, and the model learns to predict these targets for masked frames. By eliminating the learned quantizer complexity of wav2vec 2.0, BEST-RQ matched its performance with a dramatically simpler pipeline, and became a core component of Google's Universal Speech Model.

InstructGPT operationalized RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) at scale, producing a 1.3B parameter model that human raters preferred over the raw 175B GPT-3—despite being 100x smaller. This striking result proved that alignment technique could substitute for raw capacity. The three-stage recipe (supervised fine-tuning → reward model → PPO) it established became the standard for every major aligned LLM that followed, including ChatGPT.

PaLM (Pathways Language Model) was the first model trained using Google's Pathways system, spanning 6,144 TPU v4 chips in a single training job. At 540B parameters, it showed discontinuous performance jumps on BIG-bench tasks as scale increased—providing the clearest evidence yet of 'emergent abilities.' Its chain-of-thought reasoning performance set records across mathematical and commonsense reasoning, and it directly seeded PaLM 2, Gemini, and PaLM-E.

Flamingo was a milestone in multimodal AI: it connected a frozen 70B language model with a visual encoder using a Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention layers, enabling few-shot learning on image and video questions without fine-tuning. By showing that a vision encoder could be bolted onto a pre-trained LLM with minimal architectural surgery, it established the blueprint that LLaVA, GPT-4V, and nearly every subsequent visual language model followed.

Chinchilla overturned the prevailing wisdom that larger models are always better. By training a 70B model on 1.4 trillion tokens—4x more data than Gopher at the same compute budget—DeepMind showed it outperformed every model of the time including GPT-3 and Gopher. The 'Chinchilla scaling laws' redefined optimal training: for a given compute budget, train a smaller model on far more data, fundamentally changing how every subsequent model was designed.

CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) elegantly unified image-text contrastive learning (CLIP-style) with generative captioning in a single encoder-decoder framework, sharing computation between both objectives. This dual-objective design gave CoCa both discriminative embedding ability (for retrieval) and generative ability (for captioning) simultaneously—enabling state-of-the-art performance on classification, retrieval, and captioning with a single training run, with no task-specific fine-tuning required.

Meta's OPT (Open Pre-trained Transformer) was a deliberate act of transparency in the era of closed frontier models, releasing models from 125M to 175B parameters alongside full training logs, model cards, and code. By making a GPT-3-scale model fully accessible to researchers, OPT enabled reproducibility studies and safety research that was impossible with API-only access—and established Meta's pattern of open-sourcing frontier AI research.

UL2 (Unifying Language Learning) proposed a Mixture of Denoisers training objective that unified causal language modeling, masked span prediction, and extreme infilling into a single framework. Models trained with this diverse objective mixture achieved strong few-shot performance rivaling much larger models, and the 20B variant—later open-sourced by Google—became one of the most capable open-source models of its size class in 2022.

Wei et al. documented a striking phenomenon: as language models scale past certain thresholds, entirely new capabilities appear suddenly—arithmetic, analogical reasoning, multi-step problem solving—that were near-zero at smaller scales. These 'emergent abilities' challenged simple extrapolation of scaling laws and sparked an ongoing debate about whether AI progress is smooth (predictable scaling) or discontinuous (phase transitions), with profound implications for AI safety forecasting.

BIG-bench (Beyond the Imitation Game benchmark) was a collaborative effort by 450+ researchers at 132 institutions to build tasks that current models couldn't solve—from novel wordplay and causal reasoning to theory of mind and code execution. Its 204 tasks exposed both impressive emergent capabilities and stubborn failure modes, and became the most comprehensive evaluation of frontier LLMs at the time, shifting the field toward multi-task, holistic evaluation.

MetaLM from Microsoft proposed semi-causal pre-training: a bidirectional encoder and a causal language model are jointly pre-trained, with the causal model learning to generate text conditioned on encoder representations. This hybrid enabled rich contextual embeddings useful for fine-tuning while preserving the in-context learning ability of causal models, pointing toward unified architectures that could handle both discriminative and generative tasks.

PaLI (Pathways Language and Image) combined a 4B-parameter ViT image encoder with a 13B mT5 text encoder-decoder, training jointly on a mix of image-text tasks in 100+ languages. At 17B parameters, it set state-of-the-art across visual QA, captioning, and OCR tasks, and demonstrated that scaling both vision and language components together yields better results than scaling either alone—a key design principle adopted by Gemini.

DeepMind's Sparrow applied RLHF with an explicit, enumerated set of rules the model must follow, training raters to flag violations of specific safety guidelines rather than just expressing vague preference. By conditioning the reward model on rule compliance rather than general human approval, Sparrow produced a conversational agent that was measurably more factual and less harmful—an important early controlled study in rule-based AI alignment.

Flan-T5 married T5's efficient encoder-decoder architecture with the full instruction-tuning recipe scaled to 1,800+ tasks from 473 datasets—including chain-of-thought examples. The results were striking: Flan-T5-XL (3B) matched or beat GPT-3 (175B) on several benchmarks. Its open release made it the most widely used base model in academic NLP research for the following two years, enabling hundreds of downstream studies.

GLM-130B from Tsinghua University introduced a bilingual Chinese-English 130B parameter model using a unique autoregressive blank-infilling objective that outperforms both purely unidirectional and purely bidirectional pre-training. Its training recipe ran on commodity A100 GPUs using INT4 quantization from the start, making it one of the first frontier-scale models designed with deployment efficiency as a core design constraint rather than an afterthought.

HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) argued that model evaluation had become dangerously narrow—optimizing for a few benchmarks while ignoring fairness, robustness, calibration, efficiency, and toxicity. By evaluating 30 models across 42 scenarios and 7 simultaneous metrics, HELM provided the most comprehensive snapshot of the LLM landscape to date and established the case for multi-dimensional evaluation that influenced every major benchmark suite since.

BLOOM was a landmark open-science effort by 1,000+ researchers in the BigScience initiative, producing a 176B parameter multilingual model trained on 46 natural languages and 13 programming languages. Unlike GPT-3, every aspect—training data, code, and model weights—was fully open. BLOOM proved that frontier-scale multilingual models could be built entirely outside Big Tech through coordinated academic collaboration, and set the precedent for open-source frontier models that followed.

Meta trained Galactica on 48M scientific papers, textbooks, and reference materials, aiming to create a model that could navigate and synthesize scientific knowledge. While it showed impressive performance on scientific benchmarks, it was pulled from public access after just three days due to its tendency to generate confident-sounding but factually incorrect citations—an early and influential cautionary tale about hallucination risks in domain-specialized models.

ChatGPT was arguably the most consequential product launch in AI history, reaching 100 million users in two months—faster than any consumer application ever recorded. Built by applying the InstructGPT RLHF recipe to a more capable GPT-3.5 base model, it demonstrated that aligned conversational interfaces could be genuinely useful to mainstream users without any technical expertise, directly triggering the generative AI arms race among every major technology company.

OPT-IML (Instruction Meta-Learning) extended Meta's OPT models with instruction tuning across 2,000 NLP tasks, systematically studying how task diversity, filtering strategy, and sampling affect zero-shot generalization. It contributed the most comprehensive empirical evidence to date on how instruction dataset curation—which tasks to include and how to balance them—shapes a model's ability to generalize to unseen instructions.

The 2022 FLAN paper scaled instruction tuning to its logical extreme—1,800+ tasks, chain-of-thought data, and model sizes from 540M to 540B parameters—producing the definitive study of what instruction tuning can and cannot do. Its key finding: including chain-of-thought reasoning examples during instruction tuning is critical for improving held-out reasoning tasks, a conclusion that shaped every subsequent aligned model's training recipe.

Meta's LLaMA released a family of 7B–65B models trained exclusively on open-source data—no proprietary datasets. The 13B LLaMA outperformed GPT-3 (175B) on most benchmarks. By releasing weights for research use, Meta sparked an explosion of open-source fine-tuning activity that produced Alpaca, Vicuna, WizardLM, and dozens more—fundamentally democratizing LLM research and establishing the open-weight ecosystem that defines the field today.

Kosmos-1 from Microsoft trained a multimodal large language model from scratch on web-scale text, image-text pairs, and interleaved documents—without separate pre-training of vision and language components. It demonstrated strong performance on image captioning, visual question answering, and IQ tests involving visual patterns, establishing a blueprint for natively multimodal LLMs where vision and language are co-trained rather than retrofitted.

Stanford's Alpaca fine-tuned a 7B LLaMA model on 52,000 instruction-following examples generated by GPT-3.5 for under $600 total cost. Despite this minimal investment, human evaluators rated its outputs as competitive with GPT-3.5, proving that the gap between proprietary and open models could be closed by fine-tuning open weights on synthetically generated data—launching the self-instruct era of open-source LLM development.

The Linear Recurrent Unit (LRU) from DeepMind showed that a simple diagonal linear recurrence—with careful initialization and normalization—could match transformers on sequence modeling tasks at a fraction of the computational cost. By proving that the gating complexity of LSTMs was unnecessary for competitive performance, LRU pointed toward a new class of efficient recurrent architectures that scale linearly rather than quadratically with sequence length.

PaLM-E is Google's 562B parameter embodied multimodal language model—the largest vision-language model trained to date—designed to process sensor data, robot states, and visual input alongside natural language instructions. By connecting PaLM to a ViT encoder and training on robot manipulation tasks, PaLM-E demonstrated that a single large language model could serve as a general-purpose robot brain capable of planning multi-step physical tasks from language alone.

GPT-4 introduced multimodality to OpenAI's frontier and represented a substantial reasoning leap—scoring in the 90th percentile on the Bar Exam, top 10% on most standardized tests, and demonstrating advanced mathematical problem solving. Though OpenAI disclosed almost no architectural details, it was the first model good enough to replace human professionals in specific domains, and defined the performance benchmark against which all frontier models were measured for the following year.

MiniGPT-4 demonstrated that connecting a frozen BLIP-2 visual encoder to a frozen Vicuna (LLaMA-based) language model with a single learned linear projection layer was sufficient to enable conversational visual understanding. By keeping both the vision encoder and language model frozen and training only the 4M-parameter projection, MiniGPT-4 achieved impressive visual dialog at minimal compute cost, making multimodal LLM research accessible to labs without frontier-scale resources.

LLaVA (Large Language and Vision Assistant) demonstrated that connecting a CLIP vision encoder to LLaMA via a simple linear projection—trained on GPT-4-generated visual instruction data—produced a capable visual chatbot at minimal cost. This elegant, low-budget approach outperformed many purpose-built vision-language models and showed that visual instruction tuning with synthetic GPT-4 data could substitute for expensive human annotation, inspiring a generation of open-source multimodal models.

Pythia is a suite of 16 autoregressive models from 70M to 12B parameters, all trained on The Pile in exactly the same order with checkpoints saved every 1,000 steps. This unique controlled design made Pythia the first model suite enabling direct study of training dynamics across scale: researchers could observe precisely what knowledge emerges at what training step, compare identical checkpoints across different scales, and conduct mechanistic interpretability research impossible with standard one-off releases.

LIMA (Less Is More for Alignment) made a provocative and influential argument: almost all of an LLM's knowledge and capability comes from pre-training; alignment fine-tuning requires only ~1,000 carefully curated examples. A 65B LLaMA model fine-tuned on just 1,000 human-selected examples matched or outperformed models with far more RLHF training, suggesting that data quality overwhelmingly trumps quantity in alignment—a finding that fundamentally changed how practitioners approach instruction dataset construction.

Dromedary (SELF-ALIGN) showed that a useful, relatively safe assistant could be built from a base LLM using only 195 human-written principles and minimal human supervision—no preference data collection required. By having the model generate and self-critique synthetic instruction-response pairs guided by these principles, SELF-ALIGN achieved competitive performance with RLHF-trained models at a fraction of the annotation cost, pioneering the principle-based self-alignment approach.

PaLM 2 improved on PaLM not primarily by scaling but by training on a more carefully curated multilingual dataset and applying compute-optimal training recipes inspired by Chinchilla. Despite being smaller than PaLM, it outperformed it across reasoning, coding, and multilingual tasks, powering Google's Bard and Gemini API. It introduced the first public Google model family with named tiers—Gecko, Otter, Bison, Unicorn—spanning from mobile-friendly to frontier-scale.

RWKV cleverly reformulates attention as a linear recurrence computable either as a parallel transformer (for efficient training on GPUs) or as a linear-time RNN (for O(1) memory inference), eliminating the quadratic cost of attention on long sequences. It was the first model to demonstrate that RNN-style architectures could match transformer perplexity at scale, opening a practical path to training efficient recurrent models with standard transformer tooling—without sacrificing training parallelism.

Direct Preference Optimization eliminated the RL training loop from RLHF entirely: by showing that the optimal RLHF policy has a closed-form solution derivable directly from preference data, DPO enabled alignment with a simple binary cross-entropy loss. Models trained with DPO matched or outperformed PPO-trained models while being substantially simpler to implement and more stable to train—triggering rapid and near-universal adoption across the open-source LLM ecosystem within months.

Tree of Thoughts generalized chain-of-thought by allowing a language model to explore multiple reasoning paths in parallel, evaluate intermediate 'thoughts,' and backtrack when a path looks unpromising—effectively running a tree search over the model's own reasoning space. This enabled GPT-4 to solve tasks it failed at with linear chain-of-thought, like the Game of 24, by systematically exploring and pruning candidate solutions. It was a key conceptual precursor to test-time compute scaling.

Llama 2 was a genuine open-source release (research and commercial license) of 7B–70B models alongside Llama 2-Chat variants trained with over 1 million human preference annotations—more RLHF data than any publicly disclosed training at the time. By pairing competitive performance with a permissive license, it became the dominant open-source model family for over a year, the base for hundreds of community fine-tunes, and the foundation of the open-weight LLM ecosystem.

Google's Universal Speech Model (USM) trained a 2B-parameter Conformer on 12 million hours of unlabeled audio spanning 300+ languages, achieving strong speech recognition across dozens of languages including many with very little labeled training data. By combining massive unsupervised pre-training with targeted supervised fine-tuning, USM demonstrated that the pre-train-then-adapt recipe that transformed NLP could equally transform multilingual speech recognition.

Mistral 7B shattered the conventional wisdom that small models make unacceptable quality tradeoffs: using grouped-query attention (GQA) for faster inference and sliding window attention (SWA) for efficient long-context handling, the 7B model outperformed Llama 2 13B on every benchmark—while running on consumer hardware. Its Apache 2.0 release with no usage restrictions immediately made it the most popular open-source small model, spawning a thriving ecosystem of fine-tunes.

Mamba replaced the attention mechanism entirely with a selective state-space model (SSM) where transition matrices depend on the input, allowing the model to selectively remember or forget based on content. Unlike previous SSMs, it matched transformer perplexity on language modeling while scaling linearly with sequence length—making it the first serious architectural challenger to the transformer's dominance in over six years, and inspiring a new generation of hybrid SSM-attention architectures.

Mixtral 8x7B used a sparse mixture-of-experts architecture—8 experts per layer, routing each token to just 2—to pack 46.7B total parameters into a model that uses only 12.9B active parameters per token. It matched or exceeded Llama 2 70B on most benchmarks while being 6x faster at inference. Its Apache 2.0 open release made it the first open MoE to achieve genuine frontier-class performance, validating MoE as a practical architecture for open-source models.

DeepSeek-V2 combined Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)—which compresses the KV-cache by 93.3%—with a fine-grained MoE architecture (236B total, 21B active parameters) to deliver frontier performance at dramatically reduced inference cost. At $0.14/million tokens, it cost 7x less to serve than comparable models and demonstrated that MoE architectures could be practically deployed at scale, establishing DeepSeek as a serious challenger to US frontier labs.

Google's Gemma models (2B and 7B) were the first open-weight models derived from the same research and infrastructure as Gemini—trained on 6T tokens from web, code, and math sources using Gemini-class techniques. Gemma 7B outperformed comparable open models including Mistral 7B on multiple benchmarks, and its permissive commercial license combined with Gemini-class training methods demonstrated that frontier-lab insights could be packaged into models small enough for consumer hardware.

Gemini 1.5 Pro introduced a 1 million token context window—the longest of any commercially available model at the time, later extended to 2M. This was not merely a quantitative extension: Gemini 1.5 maintained near-perfect retrieval accuracy across its entire context (>99% on needle-in-a-haystack tasks at 1M tokens), making long-document analysis and large codebase reasoning practically viable for the first time.

OLMo from the Allen Institute for AI is the first truly fully open language model: weights, training data (Dolma, a 3T token curated corpus), training code, and evaluation harness are all publicly released. This rare transparency lets researchers study every aspect of modern LLM training—including data curation decisions, optimizer behavior, and the effect of specific training choices—making it the most reproducible frontier-class model ever released.

Microsoft's Phi-3-mini achieved GPT-3.5-level performance with just 3.8B parameters by training on a curated 'textbook quality' dataset—synthetic data filtered for educational value, logical structure, and code coverage. This shattered assumptions about the minimum scale needed for capable language models and demonstrated that data quality is a more powerful lever than model size, inspiring a wave of research into curriculum-based and synthetic data training strategies.

GPT-4o ('omni') unified text, audio, and vision processing in a single end-to-end trained model, replacing the previous cascade of separate models for each modality. Audio was processed directly without transcription, enabling real-time conversational latency (232ms average response time) and emotional nuance in voice responses. GPT-4o matched GPT-4 Turbo on text while being twice as fast and half the cost, and its free availability made frontier-class AI accessible to hundreds of millions of users.

Mamba 2 established a formal theoretical equivalence between structured state space models and a restricted form of linear attention, unifying two previously separate research lines. This connection enabled a simpler, 2-8x faster SSM layer and made it possible to transfer scaling insights from transformers directly to state-space models. It also introduced the State Space Dual (SSD) framework that simplifies designing future hybrid SSM-attention architectures.

Gemma 2 introduced knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model as the primary training technique for its 9B and 27B variants, along with interleaved local and global attention layers. The result was a dramatic leap over Gemma 1: Gemma 2 27B matched or outperformed Llama 3 70B on most benchmarks while being less than half the size—proving that teacher distillation could substitute for raw parameter scaling in open-source models.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet set the mid-2024 frontier, outperforming GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Claude 3 Opus on coding, reasoning, and instruction following—while being twice as fast as Opus. Anthropic also introduced the Artifacts interface for generating and running code and interactive content, and demonstrated Claude's ability to control a desktop computer through screenshot understanding, marking an early milestone in computer-use AI agents.

Llama 3 represented Meta's most ambitious open-source effort: the 70B model was trained on 15T tokens—10x more data than Llama 2—using carefully filtered multilingual web data. The 405B flagship matched GPT-4 on numerous benchmarks. Llama 3 also introduced multimodal variants and a 128K context window, demonstrating Meta's intent to match closed frontier models on every capability axis and cementing open-weight models as a genuine alternative to proprietary APIs.

FineWeb is HuggingFace's 15T token open web dataset, built by applying 10+ quality filtering and deduplication heuristics to 96 CommonCrawl snapshots. FineWeb-Edu—a 1.3T token subset filtered for educational content using a LLaMA-3-based classifier—proved to be among the highest-quality pre-training datasets ever released, enabling models trained on it to outperform those trained on proprietary web datasets including those used by Llama 3 and Mistral.

OpenAI's o1 was the first production reasoning model: rather than answering immediately, it spent time generating an internal chain of thought using reinforcement learning trained on mathematical and coding problems. By allocating test-time compute to deliberate reasoning, o1 scored in the 89th percentile on competitive programming, placed in the top 500 on the USA Math Olympiad qualifier, and achieved PhD-level accuracy on GPQA science—capabilities fundamentally beyond any prior model's reach.

OLMoE is the first fully open mixture-of-experts language model: 64 experts, 7B total parameters, 1B active per token, with all weights, training data, and code publicly released. Its performance matches or exceeds dense models 3-4x its active parameter count, and its open release provided the research community's first opportunity to study MoE training dynamics, expert routing behavior, and expert specialization patterns in full detail.

Microsoft's Phi-4 packed state-of-the-art reasoning into 14B parameters by training primarily on synthetic data: carefully generated math problems, coding exercises, and multi-step reasoning chains designed to teach systematic thinking rather than pattern matching. It outperformed Gemini Pro 1.5 and GPT-4o-mini on MATH and GPQA despite being far smaller, establishing synthetic curriculum data as a first-class alternative to scale for improving reasoning capabilities.

Qwen 2.5 is Alibaba's most capable open-weight model family to date, covering sizes from 0.5B to 72B parameters and trained on 18 trillion tokens—double Qwen 2. The 72B model set new open-source records on coding (HumanEval 86%), mathematics (MATH 83%), and instruction following, establishing Qwen as the leading open-source alternative to Llama for multilingual and coding applications, particularly in the Chinese-language AI ecosystem.

DeepSeek-V3 trained a 671B total / 37B active parameter MoE model for just $5.6M—roughly 50x less than comparable US frontier models—using FP8 mixed-precision training and Multi-Token Prediction as an auxiliary objective. It achieved performance competitive with GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and its release triggered serious industry reckoning with the efficiency gap between Chinese and US AI labs, sparking widespread discussion about AI development cost assumptions.

Gemini was Google's answer to GPT-4: the first model built natively multimodal from the ground up, trained jointly on text, images, audio, and video rather than retrofitting vision onto a language model. Its Ultra variant set state-of-the-art on 30 of 32 benchmarks and was the first model to surpass human expert performance on MMLU. Gemini's architecture introduced efficient multi-query attention and a 32K default context, establishing Google's return to frontier model leadership.

DeepSeek-R1 demonstrated that reinforcement learning alone—with no supervised chain-of-thought data—could teach a base LLM to develop sophisticated reasoning behaviors including self-verification and reflection. Starting from DeepSeek-V3, R1 developed its own emergent reasoning style through pure RL, matching OpenAI's o1 on mathematical and coding benchmarks at fully open-source weights and a fraction of the cost—shocking the industry and validating RL as a primary path to reasoning capability.

Anthropic's Claude 3 family (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) introduced the first explicitly tiered model family, with each tier optimized for a different speed-capability tradeoff. Claude 3 Opus briefly held the top position on the LMSYS chatbot arena at launch, and the family was the first to demonstrate that Constitutional AI—training models to critique and revise their own outputs against a set of principles—could produce frontier-competitive results without sacrificing safety.

Gemini 2.0 Flash marked a strategic shift from raw capability scaling to agentic capability expansion: it added native real-time audio/video output, multimodal tool use, and a 1M-token context window. Designed explicitly as an 'agentic' model capable of taking actions in the world—browsing, writing code, calling APIs—Gemini 2.0's performance-per-cost ratio made it Google's default production model across nearly all API use cases within months of release.

Claude 3.7 Sonnet introduced Anthropic's first extended thinking mode—visible, inspectable chain-of-thought reasoning that users could observe and reason about. It became the first model to surpass 60% on SWE-bench Verified (software engineering agent benchmark), demonstrating a step change in agentic code writing capability. Its hybrid design supports both immediate responses and deep deliberation, letting users trade latency for thoroughness on demand.

Gemini 2.5 Pro was Google's first thinking model, spending compute on internal chain-of-thought reasoning before responding. At launch it led GPQA, AIME 2025, and Humanity's Last Exam—the hardest AI reasoning benchmarks available—with its 1M-token context window remaining fully performant throughout. It matched or exceeded OpenAI's o1 on deliberate reasoning tasks, demonstrating that Google had closed the reasoning model gap that OpenAI had opened with o1 six months earlier.

Llama 4 marked Meta's transition to mixture-of-experts architecture, releasing Scout (17B active / 16 experts, 10M-token context—then the world's longest—fitting on a single H100) and Maverick (17B active / 128 experts, distilled from the unreleased 2T-parameter Behemoth). Both models are natively multimodal across text and images, multilingual in 12 languages, and fully open-weight, positioning Meta's open-source ecosystem as competitive with closed frontier models for the first time.

Qwen 3 introduced hybrid reasoning—models that seamlessly switch between deliberate chain-of-thought reasoning (for hard math and coding) and fast direct responses (for simple queries)—controlled via simple prompting flags. The flagship 235B-A22B MoE model competed with GPT-4.1 and Claude Sonnet 4 on reasoning benchmarks, while all models were released under Apache 2.0 and trained on 36T tokens across 119 languages, making Qwen 3 the most capable open multilingual model family at launch.

GPT-4.1 introduced a 1M-token context window and a massive leap in coding ability—54.6% on SWE-bench Verified, 21 points over GPT-4o—making it the strongest coding model available at launch. Available API-only (not in ChatGPT), it was specifically engineered for agentic software engineering workflows where enormous codebases must be held in context simultaneously, and its accompanying mini and nano variants offered the same 1M context at dramatically lower cost.

OpenAI's o3 extended the reasoning model paradigm with native tool use inside the chain of thought—the first reasoning model able to call web search, run code, and generate images as intermediate reasoning steps rather than just final outputs. It also introduced 'thinking with images,' integrating visual information directly into reasoning chains. Its safety training used 'deliberative alignment': the model reasons through its own safety guidelines before acting, rather than relying purely on trained refusal patterns.

GPT-5 unified OpenAI's two model lines—fast general intelligence and deep reasoning—into a single system that automatically routes between them based on query difficulty. It set records across every major benchmark at launch: 94.6% on AIME 2025, 74.9% on SWE-bench Verified, 84.2% on MMMU, and 46.2% on HealthBench Hard. OpenAI explicitly trained it to be less sycophantic, prioritizing accurate responses over agreeable ones—a direct response to longstanding user criticism.

xAI open-sourced Grok 1's 314B parameter mixture-of-experts weights in March 2024—the largest open-weight MoE model released at the time. Built to be integrated with real-time X (formerly Twitter) data, Grok 1 demonstrated that a newly founded lab could rapidly train frontier-scale models, and its open release surprised the industry by making a model of that scale freely available for the first time.

Grok 3 was trained on xAI's custom 200,000-GPU Memphis cluster—10x the compute of Grok 2—in a 122-day run. At launch it outperformed Claude 3.7 Sonnet and GPT-4o on AIME 2025 and GPQA Diamond benchmarks. It introduced 'Big Brain' extended-thinking mode and DeepSearch for real-time web research synthesis, demonstrating that xAI could close the capability gap with established frontier labs through sheer compute investment.

Grok 4 delivered a significant capability step over Grok 3, achieving top-tier results on frontier reasoning benchmarks including AIME and GPQA at launch. Available to SuperGrok and API subscribers, it represented xAI's most competitive frontier model to date, pushing the lab into direct competition with OpenAI o3 and Claude Opus on hard science and mathematics reasoning tasks.

Claude 4 (Opus 4 and Sonnet 4) was Anthropic's first model family with hybrid reasoning: users could choose near-instant responses or extended thinking within the same model. Opus 4 became the top coding model at launch with 72.5% on SWE-bench Verified, and both models adopted a new training approach that made Claude more direct and forthright, less prone to hedging, and better at refusing only when genuinely warranted rather than out of excessive caution.

Claude Opus 4.5 was the first AI model to break 80% on SWE-bench Verified (80.9%), establishing a new ceiling for agentic software engineering. Priced at $5/$25 per million tokens—a 67% reduction from prior Opus pricing—it made frontier-class agentic coding economically viable at scale, and introduced 'Infinite Chats' to handle multi-session context continuity in long-running autonomous workflows.

Claude Opus 4.7 is Anthropic's most capable publicly released model as of mid-2026, with a step-change improvement in agentic software engineering, 1M-token context window (beta), and stronger self-checking and instruction following. It is the first model in Anthropic's lineup to support 1M-token context in production, enabling analysis of entire large codebases and document corpora in a single pass.

Gemini 3 Pro delivered state-of-the-art reasoning and multimodal capability at launch, introducing 'Deep Think' mode that iterates through multiple hypotheses simultaneously for hard math, science, and logic problems. It was the first Gemini model with native Computer Use tool support, enabling it to control a browser or desktop autonomously. Gemini 3 Flash, released simultaneously, achieved frontier-class performance rivaling larger models while remaining fast and cost-efficient.

Gemini 3.1 Pro pushed Google's frontier further with a 77.1% score on ARC-AGI-2—one of the hardest general reasoning benchmarks—and significant improvements in complex multi-step agentic workflows. As Google's most capable publicly available model as of early 2026, it underscored DeepMind's strategy of tight iteration cycles between flagship Pro releases and efficiency-focused Flash variants.

Mistral 3 (Mistral Large 3) was a coordinated 10-model release including a 675B total / 41B active parameter MoE flagship with 256K context window, multimodal capability, and Apache 2.0 licensing—plus nine compact Ministral edge models. Its open-weight frontier release represented Mistral's most serious challenge yet to closed proprietary APIs, closing the gap with GPT-4-class models while remaining fully open for commercial use.

Phi-4-reasoning demonstrated that a 14B open-weight model trained with reinforcement learning could compete with models many times its size on math and science reasoning benchmarks. By combining Phi-4's high-quality synthetic training data with RL fine-tuning, it showed that reasoning capability—previously thought to require frontier-scale models—could be unlocked in small, efficient, deployable models, making strong reasoning accessible on edge hardware.

GPT-5.5 is OpenAI's most capable publicly available model as of May 2026, described as their 'smartest and most intuitive' release. GPT-5.5 Instant—the free-tier default from May 5, 2026—reduces hallucinations by 52.5% versus its predecessor on high-stakes domains including law, medicine, and finance, and can reference past conversations and integrate with Gmail for personalized responses, blurring the line between assistant and persistent agent.
